input.prompt:
My MSTest project has a .testsettings file that configures test deployment, timeout,
and a code coverage data collector. After upgrading MSTest packages, the settings
seem to be ignored. How do I migrate this configuration?

data.content:

and a code coverage data collector. After upgrading MSTest packages, the settings
seem to be ignored. How do I migrate this configuration?

data.reasoningText: The user has an MSTest project with a `.testsettings` file and wants to migrate it to the modern `.runsettings` format. Let me look at their project and testsettings file to understand what they have.

arguments.file_text:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<RunSettings>
  <RunConfiguration>
    <TestSessionTimeout>30000</TestSessionTimeout>
  </RunConfiguration>
  <MSTest>
    <AssemblyResolution>
      <Directory useLoadContext="true" />
    </AssemblyResolution>
  </MSTest>
  <DataCollectionRunSettings>
    <DataCollectors>
      <DataCollector friendlyName="Code Coverage"
        uri="datacollector://microsoft/CodeCoverage/2.0"
        assemblyQualifiedName="Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector.CoverageCollectorDataCollector, Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector, Culture=neutral" />
    </DataCollectors>
  </DataCollectionRunSettings>
</RunSettings>

data.content:
The `.testsettings` format is **legacy** and no longer supported by modern MSTest (v3+). You need to migrate to a `.runsettings` file. I've created `local.runsettings` that maps your three settings:

| `.testsettings` setting | `.runsettings` equivalent |
|---|---|
| `TestTimeout` property (30s) | `<TestSessionTimeout>` in `<RunConfiguration>` |
| `AssemblyResolution` / `useLoadContext` | `<AssemblyResolution>` in `<MSTest>` |
| Code Coverage data collector | `<DataCollectors>` in `<DataCollectionRunSettings>` |

**To activate it**, either:
1. Pass it at the CLI: `dotnet test --settings local.runsettings`
2. Or add it to your `.csproj`:
   ```xml
   <PropertyGroup>
     <RunSettingsFilePath>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\local.runsettings</RunSettingsFilePath>
   </PropertyGroup>
   ```

You can then safely delete `local.testsettings`.
